content:
Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells Hyeongrae Cho a, Esra Tas a

, Julian Seiler a,b, Petia Atanasova c, Vladimir Atanasov a,* a Institute of Chemical Process Engineering, University of Stuttgart, 70199, Stuttgart, Germanyb Deutsches Zentrum für Luftund Raumfahrt (DLR), Institute of Thermodynamics, 70569, Stuttgart, Germanyc Institute for Materials Science, University of Stuttgart, 70569, Stuttgart, Germany

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: A B S T R A C T This study presents ion-pair membranes based on commercially available poly(4-vinylpyridine) (PVPyr), which underwent a simple and quantitative N-methylation to form methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) (Me-PVPyr). The high charge density of Me-PVPyr resulted in brittleness, which precluded the formation of a me­chanically stable polymer membrane. In order to overcome this issue, polybenzimidazole (F6PBI), a polymer with good film-forming properties and thermal stability, was blended with the Me-PVPyr. An optimized blend ratio of 7/3 wt % (Me-PVPyr/F6PBI, Blend 7/3) demonstrated excellent proton conductivity of 409 mS cm-1 at 180 ◦C under anhydrous conditions, surpassing that of commercially available PA-doped pPBI membranes being 366 mS cm-1. Furthermore, the blend membranes demonstrated superior conductivity retention during thermal cycling between 80 ◦C (50 % RH) and 160 ◦C (0 % RH). In the fuel cell, the Blend 7/3 membrane reached a peak power density of 1.69 W cm-2 at 200 ◦C with 2 bars of back pressure in H2/O2, outperforming commercial pPBI membranes under the same test conditions. An accelerated stress test (AST) involving thermal cycling between 80 and 160 ◦C at a constant voltage (0.5V) demonstrated the superior durability of the blend membrane, which showed no performance decay over 100 cycles, in contrast to the significant decay of pPBI within 70 cycles.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: or durability of the blend membrane, which showed no performance decay over 100 cycles, in contrast to the significant decay of pPBI within 70 cycles. A post-mortem analysis of Blend 7/3 membrane electrode assembly (MEA) by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) revealed a decrease in membrane thickness from 60 μm to 15 μm during the fuel cell operation, accompanied by minimal creeping of the membrane into the catalyst layers.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: 1. Introduction Humanity is confronted by a significant challenge considering the advancing climate change and increasing of CO2 levels. With the simultaneous global increase in energy demand, it is necessary to in­crease the share of renewable energy sources. To achieve this, electricity is increasingly generated by sustainably and in a climate neutral manner from decentralized renewable sources such as solar, wind and hydro­power [1]. To counteract the fluctuations in energy generation caused by daily and seasonal variations as well as weather conditions, powerful energy storage systems are indispensable. One of the promising strate­gies is the utilization of material energy storage, which store electrical energy in their chemical bonds and exhibit a particularly high energy density. Hydrogen obtained from electrolysis plays a pivotal role due to its high mass-specific energy density, as it can be easily converted back into electric power in fuel cells through electrochemical reactions [2]. Since the fuel cells energy efficiency is comparatively high, the process is locally emission-free and the conversion of primary energy into heat as an intermediate step to useable energy is avoided, the hydrogen-powered fuel cell is a good alternative to heat engines and holds a central significance in our energy supply [3].

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Currently, polymer electrolyte membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) have gained considerable interest due to advantages such as improved cell design, operating modes and high-power densities [4]. Research on this type of fuel cell and its components is a promising field as it represents more environmentally friendly alternatives to internal combustion en­gines for various mobile and stationary applications. The optimization of membranes in the so-called membrane electrode assemblies (MEAs), where the reaction takes place, is of particular interest as they signifi­cantly influence the performance of the fuel cell [5]. The introduced in the late 1960s by DuPont polymer electrolyte membrane Nafion, which is a perfluorosulfonic acid (PFSA) -based cation exchange membrane is the most widely studied one due to its high ionic conductivity and chemical stability [6]. Since the proton conduction of the PFSA is strongly influenced by the water content, efficient operation with such PFSA-based membranes is limited by temperatures below 100 ◦C. Other drawbacks of low temperature operating using PFSA-based membranes require cooling systems and constant humidification [7]. However, operating within higher temperature ranges could enhance the oxygen reduction reaction and elevate tolerance against gas contamination in the electrodes [8]. This inspires the development of desirable mem­branes for high-temperature fuel cells. A commonly employed

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Available online 23 December 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). high-temperature fuel cell membrane is the phosphoric acid (PA) doped polybenzimidazole (PBI) membrane, which can effectively operate at temperatures up to 180 ◦C [9]. PA-doped membrane provides proton migration pathway from the anode to the cathode side. However, an issues in PA-PBI system is related to PA loss at high current density (PA extraction by water) and high temperatures and gas flows on the elec­trodes (PA evaporation) resulting in enhanced performance decay after few thousand hours in operation [10]. More recent research of YS Kim et al. revealed that the use of permanently charged (ion-pair) polymers in the membrane can effi­ciently adsorb PA [11].This was explained with increased interaction energy between charged polymer and biphosphate ions (ion-pair inter­action), which has been 6 times higher than the interaction energy be­tween PBI and PA (acid-base interaction). The authors have demonstrated a stable performance of the ion-pair membrane under cycling temperature conditions from 80 to 160 ◦C for about 500 cycles. In a subsequent collaboration, we developed gas diffusion electrodes (GDE) with a phosphonated poly(pentafluorostyrene) (PWN) as a cata­lyst binder and poly(phenylene)-based ion-pair membrane in a mem­brane electrode assembly (MEA).

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: phosphonated poly(pentafluorostyrene) (PWN) as a cata­lyst binder and poly(phenylene)-based ion-pair membrane in a mem­brane electrode assembly (MEA). This MEA displayed more stable performance than a PA-PBI system in fuel cell operated for about 550 h at 160 ◦C and an outstanding peak power density of 1.7 W cm-2 at 240 ◦C [12]. Addition of Nafion as a co-binder in the catalyst layer has further enhanced the performance of the fuel cell due to protonation of the PWN [13]. In this paper, we report an ion-pair polyelectrolyte based on com­mercial poly(4-vinylpyridine) (PVPyr) for high temperature fuel cell applications. To achieve versatile and quantitative conversion of PVPyr to ion-pair polyelectrolyte, post modification via N-methylation qua­ternization reaction with iodomethane was utilized. This polymer analogous reaction has been already reported for PVPyr [14]. The thermal stability of the N-methylated PVPyr has been found to be outstanding and the polymer has remained stable in an acid condition and therefore suitable for vanadium redox flow battery application [15]. These advantageous properties motivated us to apply N-methylated PVPyr as polylelectrolyte membrane for high temperature PEMFC (HT-PEMFC). Conversion of PVPyr to ion-pair polymer was aiming to enhance the adsorption of the PA on a charged polymer backbone.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: gh temperature PEMFC (HT-PEMFC). Conversion of PVPyr to ion-pair polymer was aiming to enhance the adsorption of the PA on a charged polymer backbone. The better adsorption of PA on ion-pair system was confirmed experimen­tally by applying water extraction of PA from the obtained membranes via temperature and relative humidity cycling and recording impedance and fuel cell performance. Thus, beside its high thermal and mechanical stability, membranes based on N-methylated, quaternized PVPyr showed superior ionic conductivity (409 mS cm-1 at 180 ◦C), power density (1.69 W cm-2 at 200 ◦C) and durability (stable performance at 100 cycles 80–160 ◦C in the FC). 2. Experimental 2.1. Materials Poly(4-vinylpyridine) (PVPyr) (average Mw ~160,000) and iodo­methane were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A polybenzimidazole with hexafluoro isopropylidene moiety (F6-PBI) was obtained from Yan Jin Technology Co., Ltd. Tianjin. A PA-doped pPBI membrane (CelltechP) was provided by BASF. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sulfuric acid, potassium hydroxide, 0.1 N standard hydrochloric acid, sodium chloride and sodium hydroxide were pur­chased from Carl Roth GmbH. Phosphoric acid (PA) and diethyl ether were purchased by VWR international GmbH. 2.2. Synthesis of methylated PVPyr (Me-PVPyr) For complete methylation: PVPyr (5g, 47.6 mmol) was dissolved in 250 ml NMP in a 500 ml round bottom flask equipped with argon in/outlet and condenser.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: or complete methylation: PVPyr (5g, 47.6 mmol) was dissolved in 250 ml NMP in a 500 ml round bottom flask equipped with argon in/outlet and condenser. After complete dissolution of PVPyr in NMP, iodomethane (6.75g, 47.6 mmol) was added into the polymer solution. The reaction temperature was kept at 90 ◦C for 18 h. The polymer was obtained by precipitation of the reaction solution into diethyl ether. The obtained product was washed with diethyl ether several times. Subse­quently, the final polymer was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 ◦C for 24 h. Yield: 9.30g (79 %). Degree of methylation (DoM) was calculated from the integrals of the corresponding signals in NMR spectra using a Bruker Avance 400 MHz spectrometer. 2.3. Membrane preparation Two polymer solutions were prepared separately: A 10 wt % MePVPyr (50 % DoM) polymer solution was prepared by dissolving the polymer in NMP at RT for 24 h. A 5 wt % F6-PBI polymer solution was prepared by dissolving the polymer in NMP at 80 ◦C for 24 h. The two polymer solutions were mixed at RT for 2 h to result in polymer mixtures with ratios from 9/1 to 6/4 wt (Me-PVPyr/F6PBI). The mixed polymer solution was carefully poured into a glass plate and casted using a doctor blade. The glass plate was placed in a convection oven at 60 ◦C for 24 h to evaporate the solvent. Then, the glass plate was immersed in a deionized water bath in order to peel off the membrane. The resulting wet blend membrane was kept in a zipper bag prior to further use. 2.4.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: rsed in a deionized water bath in order to peel off the membrane. The resulting wet blend membrane was kept in a zipper bag prior to further use. 2.4. Water uptake The water uptake was determined by comparing the membrane weight of the wet and dry states. For this purpose, a membrane sample was dried at 60 ◦C for 1 day in an oven and its weight was measured (Wd). The dry membrane was immersed in deionized water for 24 h at RT. After then, the weight of the wetted membrane (Ww) was measured after removal of residual water by blotting with a tissue paper on the surfaces. The water uptake was determined using the following equation. Water uptake[WU, %] = 100 ∗(Ww -Wd)Wd(Eq. 1) 2.5. Thermogravimetric analysis To investigate the thermal resistance, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with the NETZSCH STA 449 F3 Jupiter [16]. A dry sample of each polymer or membrane was placed in a crucible pan and positioned in a heating chamber. The weight changes with increasing the temperature were recorded. The temperature was increased from 30 to 600 ◦C with a heating rate of 20 K min-1 under gas mixture of O2 and N2 (O2: 56 mL min-1, N2: 24 mL min-1). 2.6. Phosphoric acid doping and dimensional stability The membranes were dried at 60 ◦C for 24 h prior to PA doping, then dried weight was measured (Wundoped). Then membrane samples were immersed in an 85 wt % phosphoric acid for 1 day at different tem­peratures.

The weight of PA-doped membranes (Wdoped) was measured after careful removal of excess PA on the surfaces with a tissue paper. The PA uptake (PAU, %) was calculated as follow: PA uptake[PAU, %] = 100 *(wdoped -wundoped

) Ldoped (Eq. 3) where Ldoped and Lundoped were the length of the PA doped membrane and dry membrane, respectively. 2.7. Ion exchange capacity Ion exchange capacity (IEC) was determined by an acid-base back titration [17]. An undoped blend membrane was immersed in 1 M po­tassium hydroxide (KOH) to convert iodide to hydroxide at RT for 24 h. The membranes were then washed with deionized water intensively to remove the excess KOH on the membrane surfaces followed by soaking it in 60 ml of saturated sodium chloride (NaCl) aqueous solution for 24 h at RT. Subsequently, 3 mL of a standard 0.1 N hydrochloric acid (HCl) solution was added to the saturated NaCl solution and kept overnight. Then the membranes were washed with deionized water and this water was transferred to saturated NaCl solution. The back titration was con­ducted with a 0.1 N sodium hydroxide (NaOH) standard solution using bromothymol blue as an indicator. Then membranes were washed with deionized water intensively and dried at 60 ◦C in an oven for overnight. Then the dry weight of membranes was determined. The total IEC was calculated using following equation. IEC(mmol g-1)= VHCl * CHCl -VNaOH * CNaOH mdry

(Eq. 3)

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Where CHCl is the concentration of a HCl solution (mmol ml-1), VHCl is the used volume of a HCl solution (ml), CNaOH is the concentration of a NaOH solution (mmol ml-1), VNaOH is the added volume of a NaOH solution (ml) and mdry is the weight of the membrane after drying (g). 2.8. Proton conductivity The proton conductivities were measured through impedance spec­troscopy with two different measurement conditions. First, conductivity of membrane was measured under fully hydrated environment in 1 M sulfuric acid with IM6 impedance potentiostat from Zahner Elektrik (Kronach, Germany) as reported in the literature [17]. This measure­ment allows quick and reliable comparative conductivity and provides useful information for other energy application such as vanadium redox flow battery. Undoped membrane sample was conditioned by immersing it in 1 M sulfuric acid for 1 day at RT. Then the conditioned membrane was sandwiched between conditioned FAP 450 (Fumatech GmbH) membranes. The impedance of the membrane was recorded in a fre­quency range from 1 kHz to 4 or 8 MHz in 1 M sulfuric acid at RT. The impedance of the sample was obtained by subtracting the impedance of both FAP 450 membranes from the one of the sandwiched samples. The second impedance measurement was carried out using Mem­brane Test System (MTS 740, operating temperature up to 180 ◦C) from Scribner Associates with impedance analyzer (Solartron A 1260).

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: ied out using Mem­brane Test System (MTS 740, operating temperature up to 180 ◦C) from Scribner Associates with impedance analyzer (Solartron A 1260). A PAdoped membrane was positioned between two gas diffusion layers (GDL) attached to the platinum electrodes using a conductive carbon glue. The cell assembly was then placed in a climate chamber. The impedance was recorded under anhydrous conditions. The sample was first conditioned by heating at 120 ◦C for 5 h to remove the absorbed water from the membrane. Then, the measurement was started at 120 ◦C by decreasing the temperature to 40 ◦C, followed by heating up to 180 ◦C with impedance measurements conducted at an interval of 20 ◦C. The impedance under thermal cycling: 80 ◦C (50 % RH) and 160 ◦C (dry) was carried out using MTS 740, Scribner Associates. The test sample was equilibrated at 120 ◦C for 5 h followed by 100 temperature cycles at 160 ◦C (dry) for 2 h and 80 ◦C (50 % RH) for 2 h. Impedance was measured subsequent to the equilibration of the sample at each temperature. The conductivity was calculated according to the following equation. σ = 1Rsp =dR * A(Eq. 4) Where σ is conductivity (mS cm-1), Rsp is resistivity (Ω cm), d is the thickness of the membranes (cm), R is ohmic resistance (Ω), and A is the electrode dimension (cm2). 2.9. Tensile strength Tensile strength of PA-doped blend membranes was investigated using TMA 420 F1 equipment from Netsch.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: electrode dimension (cm2). 2.9. Tensile strength Tensile strength of PA-doped blend membranes was investigated using TMA 420 F1 equipment from Netsch. Each membrane was cut with a sharp blade into pieces 1.5 cm in length and 0.5 cm in width. The PA-doped membrane was placed between two clamps. The tensile strength test was carried out applying an elongation speed of 0.01 mm min-1 at 30 ◦C and under a nitrogen stream. At least two samples of each material were measured. 2.10. Membrane electrode assembly preparation and test in fuel cell A membrane electrode assembly (MEA) was fabricated prior to FC test. The MEA preparation and fuel cell testing were conducted as pre­viously reported [18]. The PA-doped membrane with active area of 4 cm2 was sandwiched without hot pressing between commercially available GDEs from Celtec P1100W with Pt alloy of 1 mg cm-2 on the anode and 0.7 mg cm-2 on the cathode. The MEA was then assembled with two graphite end plates incorporating gas flow channels. The assembled single cell was tested using the Fuel Cell Test Systems 850e (operating temperature up to 200 ◦C) from Scribner Associates. Fuel cell performance was characterized applying various operating conditions and measurement methods. The operation temperature was changed in the range from 80 ◦C to 200 ◦C, while the gas flow stoichi­ometry on anode and cathode sides was set in the range of 1.2–2.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: temperature was changed in the range from 80 ◦C to 200 ◦C, while the gas flow stoichi­ometry on anode and cathode sides was set in the range of 1.2–2. For the polarization curve measurement, the open-circuit voltage (OCV) was measured first in analog mode. Current density was measured at 0.05 V intervals, from OCV down to 0.1V and then back to the OCV. In-situ impedance spectroscopy was performed using a Zahner ZENNIUM XC galvanostat connected to the fuel cell test station during operation. Data for potential, impedance, and phase were collected over a frequency range from 80 kHz to 0.5 Hz and analysed using Nyquist plots. Impedance measurements were conducted at ambient pressure and at temperatures of 160, 180, and 200 ◦C. Acceleration stress test (AST) for the fuel cell was done after recording the polarization curves at different temperatures and elec­trode back pressures. Then the cell voltage was set at 0.5 V and the temperature was cycled between 160 ◦C (2 h) and 80 ◦C (2 h) with dry gasses, 1 atm ab pressure on the electrodes and gas flow stoichiometry λ H2/air = 2/4. The same conditions were used for recording the polari­zation curves at temperatures 20–200 ◦C with equilibration time of 1h at each temperature. 2.11. MEA post-mortem analysis by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis For the MEA cross-sectional analysis in SEM, MEA samples were cut across the plane with a razor blade.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: EM) with energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis For the MEA cross-sectional analysis in SEM, MEA samples were cut across the plane with a razor blade. The cross-section samples were sputtered (ACE600, Leica) prior to their imaging with 5 nm iridium. The analysis was performed using Zeiss Merlin SEM device equipped with Oxford Instruments AZtecLive Advanced EDX (Ultim Extreme Detector). The EDX mapping was accomplished at operating voltage of 5 kV. The micrographs were recorded using Inlens detector operating at a voltage of 3 kV. 3. Results and discussions Currently, the state-of-the-art membranes for HT-PEMFC are based

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: H. Cho et al. Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091 on so called ion-pair polymers [11]. These membranes are composed of permanently positively charged polyelectrolytes bearing a biphosphate counterion and surrounded by associated phosphoric acids and water molecules. These molecules interact with the ion-pair polymers via hydrogen bonds and electrostatic interactions between the charges and the polarized molecules. The augmentation of the interaction energy between the polyelectrolytes and the surrounding phosphoric acid and water, in comparison to the so-called acid-base interaction, for instance, polybenzimidazole-phosphoric acid, exerted a favorable influence on the phosphoric acid retention within the membrane and diminished acid leaching from the cell. To gain access to ion-pair polymers, we opted to utilize a commer­cially available polymer that can be readily converted into a perma­nently charged polymer through a straightforward and versatile modification reaction involving N-methylation with iodomethane. 3.1. Methylation of PVPyr and structural analysis To convert PVPyr to an ion-pair polymer, N-methylation in pyridine ring was applied. PVPyr was methylated using iodomethane as shown in Fig. 1 (a). Methylation of tertiary amines to quaternary ammoniums using iodomethane is a well-established reaction [19,20]. The degree of methylation (DoM) can be controlled either by adjusting the equivalent of iodomethane or by varying the reaction time [21].

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: ion [19,20]. The degree of methylation (DoM) can be controlled either by adjusting the equivalent of iodomethane or by varying the reaction time [21]. The DoM (%) was calculated according to the literature by comparing the aromatic inte­gration ratio of methylated aromatic protons to the total integration of aromatic protons [14]. In this study, DoM was controlled by adjusting the equivalent of iodomethane to PVPyr. First, the use of 1 equivalent of iodomethane to PVPyr reacted at 90 ◦C for 24 h led to the complete conversion, i.e. a 100 % DoM of PVPyr. The DoM was obtained from the 1HNMR spectra of the products (Fig. 1 (c)). The resonance signals at 8.2 and 6.7 ppm are attributed to the aromatic protons of PVPyr. After full methylation, these signals were downfield-shifted to 8.8 and 7.7 ppm, respectively. This indicated a complete conversion of pyridine to N-methyl pyridinium. Additionally, a new peak at 4.3 ppm appeared in the spectra of the products, attributed to the protons of the N-methyl group of Me-PVPyr. When 0.5 equivalent of iodomethane was used, a 50 % DoM was obtained from the integral ratio of methylated and non-methylated PVPyr in the 1HNMR (Fig. 1 (c) top). 3.2. Blend membrane preparation and properties The main characterization properties regarding the electrochemical application of the membranes are listed in Table 1. PVPyr that has un­dergone full methylation (100 % Me-PVPyr) possesses a high ion ex­change capacity (IECcalc = 4.05 mmol g-1).

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: are listed in Table 1. PVPyr that has un­dergone full methylation (100 % Me-PVPyr) possesses a high ion ex­change capacity (IECcalc = 4.05 mmol g-1). It is water soluble, brittle, and incapable of forming a self-standing membrane. A 50 % reduction in the DoM (50 % Me-PVPyr) yielded a polymer with an IECcalc = 2.84 mmol g-1. However, the polymer remained brittle, and the membranes exhibited inadequate mechanical stability. In order to enhance the membrane's stability and durability, a polybenzimidazole derivative known as F6PBI is incorporated during the preparation of blend mem­branes. F6PBI (structure in Fig. 1(b)) was selected due to its mechanical robustness and chemical stability in an acidic environment [16]. Stable blend membranes were obtained by blending 50 % Me-PVPyr with F6PBI at mass ratios of 8:2, 7:3 and 6:4 for 50 % Me-PVPyr: F6PBI. The blend membrane with a mass ratio 9:1 was found to be mechanically unstable after solvent evaporation and broken into pieces, while the bland membrane with a mass ratio of 8:2 (Blend 8/2) was mechanically stable and showed the highest IEC = 4.7 mmol g-1 due to the highest content of 50 % Me-PVPyr. The IEC = 4.3 and 3.9 mmol g-1 were ob­tained for membranes with mass ratios 7:3 (Blend 7/3) and 6:4 (Blend

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Fig. 1. (a) Reaction scheme of N-methylation on PVPyr; (b) Structure of F6PBI used for blend membranes; (c) 1H NMR spectra of PVPyr (bottom), 100 % Me-PVPyr (middle) and 50 % Me-PVPyr (top); (d) TGA profiles of PVPyr, 100 % Me-PVPyr and 50 % Me-PVPyr; (e) TGA profiles of the blend membranes (8/2, 7/3 and 6/4 wt 50 % Me-PVPyr/F6PBI). H. Cho et al. Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: 6/4) respectively. These results suggested that a minimum of 20 % F6PBI is required to provide sufficient chain entanglement for me­chanical stability of the Me-PVPyr based blend membranes. The measured IEC values of the blend membranes were slightly higher than the IECcalc of 50 % Me-PVPyr. This deviation can be explained by the possible formation of ionic crosslinking between deprotonated imid­azole (imidazolate) from PBI and pyridinium cation from Me-PVPyr during the ion exchanging process in KOH aqueous solution as re­ported in the literature [22,23]. Thus, during the IEC determination, the blend membranes in OH-form is exposed in sodium chloride solution and addition of HCl, this HCl not only replaces the hydroxide ions but also can break the ionic crosslinking between imidazolate and pyr­idinium. This additional consumption of HCl may explain the higher experimental IEC than the calculated one. Nevertheless, despite this deviation, apparent trends in IECs were observed in related properties such as water uptake and phosphoric acid uptake, indicating the validity of the related properties. Despite their comparatively high IEC values, all blend membranes demonstrated mechanical stability in their wet states. This phenomenon might be attributed to the formation of inter­penetrating polymer networks with F6PBI, as well as the numerous ionic, hydrogen-bond, and entanglement interactions between both polymers [16,24].

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: 3.3. Thermal stability Blending of polymers to gain more interactions and stabilize the membrane has an impact on the membrane's chemical and thermal resistance. First, the thermal stability of PVPyr and 100 and 50 % MePVPyr polymers was investigated by TGA analysis (Fig. 1 (d)). The pristine PVPyr polymer maintained its thermal stability up to 315 ◦C. After methylation, a decrease in the thermal stability was observed. The initial weight loss around 100 ◦C can be attributed to water evaporation and the weight loss around 260 ◦C can be explained by a demethylation reaction caused by nucleophilic attack of iodide on pyridinium, as already reported in the literature and characteristic for anion exchange polymers [21]. In Fig. 1 (e), the thermal stabilities of the 50 % Me-PVPyr/F6PBI blend membranes prepared with different mass ratios are presented. As with pure polymers, this initial weight loss at approximately 100 ◦C can be attributed to water evaporation. The blending of F6PBI, which exhibits high thermal stability, with other components is intended to enhance the overall thermal stability of the blend. This is achieved by mitigating chain entanglement and fostering additional interactions [25]. Indeed, when Me-PVPyr was blended with the thermally stable F6PBI, the decomposition of the blend membranes (weight loss around 260–270 ◦C) was slowed down compared to the pure Me-PVPyr poly­mer.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: ermally stable F6PBI, the decomposition of the blend membranes (weight loss around 260–270 ◦C) was slowed down compared to the pure Me-PVPyr poly­mer. A more thorough examination of the TGA profiles indicated that Blend 8/2 exhibited marginally superior thermal stability, with a degradation temperature onset of 275 ◦C. This was followed by Blend 7/3, which demonstrated a degradation temperature onset of 270 ◦C, and Blend 6/4, with a degradation temperature onset of 255 ◦C. It was demonstrated that all blend membranes exhibit sufficient thermal stability within the requisite operating temperature range for high-temperature fuel cells applications. It is important to acknowledge that the thermal stability of the blend membranes can be further enhanced by converting the iodide form of the Me-PVPyr to the corre­sponding biphosphates through PA doping and subsequent exchange of the counterions [18]. 3.4. Water and phosphoric acid uptake One way to verify the membrane stabilization by increased interac­tion with additional blending polymer is to study the water uptake (WU) of the prepared membranes. The WU of the blend membranes with different mass ratios was determined by immersing the membrane in water at RT and 60 ◦C for 24 h respectively (Fig. 2 (a)).

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: f the blend membranes with different mass ratios was determined by immersing the membrane in water at RT and 60 ◦C for 24 h respectively (Fig. 2 (a)). It is anticipated that the high concentration of ionic groups present within the polymer electrolytes will result in significant swelling of the membrane in an aqueous environment, potentially leading to a deterioration in its me­chanical integrity. The experimental results, however, demonstrated that augmenting the Me-PVPyr content in the blend membranes (mass ratios 6/4, 7/3, and 8/2) resulted in a concomitant increase in WU and an enhancement in IEC. Notwithstanding the augmented WU, the membranes exhibited unbroken mechanical integrity, devoid of any signs of brittleness. In addition, phosphoric acid uptake (PAU) is one of the most important parameters that impact on both proton conductivity and fuel cell performance (Fig. 2 (b)). PAU of the membranes strongly depends on the doping conditions such as doping time, temperature and PA concentration [26]. Based on literature reports, PA doping equilibrium in ion-pair polymer membranes can typically be achieved within 20 h at RT [27]. In this study, doping of the blend membranes was conducted in an 85 % PA solution for 24 h at different temperatures (4 ◦C, RT and 60 ◦C). As illustrated in Fig. 2 (b), the optimal PA doping conditions, particularly the doping temperature, exhibit a high degree of variability and cannot be universally applied.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: 2 (b), the optimal PA doping conditions, particularly the doping temperature, exhibit a high degree of variability and cannot be universally applied. In comparison with WU, where the Blend 8/2 exhibited the highest uptake at both RT and 60 ◦C, the highest PAU in 85 % PA for 24 h was attained with the Blend 6/4 at 60 ◦C, amounting to more than 500 %. At this temperature, the Blend 8/2 exhibits the poorest uptake, which can be attributed to leaching of the membrane due to its elevated ion-pair polymer content. In contrast, the Blend 8/2 demonstrated optimal up­take at RT, while the Blend 7/3 yielded the most favorable outcomes at 4 ◦C. These results suggest that the optimized PA doping conditions should be identified for each specific membrane composition. 3.5. Ex-situ proton conductivity and activation energy A relatively quick measurement of conductivity at RT was carried out for all investigated blend membranes in a 1 M sulfuric acid solution (Table 1). As expected, owing to its high IEC, the Blend 8/2 demon­strated the highest conductivity of 129 mS cm-1 compared to conduc­tivities of 71 and 27 mS cm-1 measured for Blend 7/3 and Blend 6/4, respectively. This increase in conductivity correlates with the higher ion exchange capacity of the membranes. As the Me-PVPyr content in­creases, charge density and adsorbed phosphoric acid also increase, leading to enhanced proton transport and improved conductivity. Proton conductivity of Me-PVPyr/F6PBI blend membranes PA-doped

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Blend 8/24.6527026.631334129421Blend 7/34.2927019.82922871409Blend 6/43.9426014.0112222755pPBI3–––~1000––366 H. Cho et al. Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091 in an 85 % PA solution at RT for 24 h was investigated and compared with commercial PA-doped PBI (pPBI) under anhydrous conditions (Fig. 2 (c)). Following the equilibration of the membrane at 120 ◦C for 5 h, the impedance was measured down to RT and then up to 180 ◦C. Comparable conductivity for blend membranes was indicated when compared with a commercial pPBI membrane. The conductivity trend followed the blend compositions, Blend 8/2 exhibited the highest con­ductivity, followed by Blend 7/3 and Blend 6/4, corresponding to the increased content of Me-PVPyr in the blend. This is also in line with the PAU results indicating the order of PAU: 6/4 (112 %) -> 7/3 (292 %) ->8/2 (313 %). The blend membrane of 8/2 showed slightly higher con­ductivity than that of pPBI membrane and 7/3 membranes showed comparable conductivity to pPBI membrane at all temperatures, indi­cating 421, 409 and 367 mS cm-1 for Blend 8/2, Blend 7/3 and pPBI respectively at 180 ◦C. It should be noted that the conductivity of pPBI was assessed based on commercially available samples, which PA doping would be highly optimized. The Blend 8/2 membranes exhibited slightly higher conductivity than that of pPBI membrane even with a lower PAU of 313 % compared to ~1000 % of pPBI.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: The Blend 8/2 membranes exhibited slightly higher conductivity than that of pPBI membrane even with a lower PAU of 313 % compared to ~1000 % of pPBI. Comparable con­ductivity was also reported in the literature for ion pair membrane (PVC19 % APIm) showing 257 mS cm-1 with 301 % PAU at 180 ◦C [28]. Nevertheless, Blend 8/2 membranes showed higher conductivity which might be due to the membrane thinning caused by the electrode compression during the EIS measurements. Membrane thinning was also observed in post-mortem analysis after fuel cell durability tests (see Fig. 4 and discussion). The activation energy (Ea) for each membrane was calculated from the slope of the Arrhenius plot shown in Fig. 2 (d). The Ea decreases when the phosphoric acid content increases [29]. Accordingly, the calculated Ea for blend membranes decreased with increasing Me-PVPyr content, which corresponds to the increased PAU as following: Blend 6/4 (34.5 kJ mol-1) > Blend 7/3 (29.7 kJ mol-1) > Blend 8/2 (25.3 kJ mol-1). However, despite the pPBI membrane exhibiting a much higher PAU than the Blend 8/2 membrane, their Ea values were comparable (pPBI: 25.1 kJ mol-1 and Blend 8/2: 25.3 kJ mol-1). This finding sug­gests that the methylated PVPyr utilized in this study may exhibit a more efficient proton conduction mechanism compared to that of the pPBI, thereby compensating for the lower PAU of blend membranes relative to pPBI.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: a more efficient proton conduction mechanism compared to that of the pPBI, thereby compensating for the lower PAU of blend membranes relative to pPBI. Even though Blend 8/2 membrane showed highest proton conduc­tivity at the elevated temperature by the ex-situ test, the Blend 8/2 membrane did not show proper performance in FC exhibiting low OCV (lower than 0.5V). This is due to that the Blend 8/2 membrane showed lowest dimensional stability with a 34 % reduction in length (see Table 1). In comparison the Blend 7/3 and Blend 6/4 membranes showed lower dimensional reductions of 28 and 22 % respectively. The lowest dimensional stability of Blend 8/2 may explain its relatively low OCV in the FC tests suggesting high gas-crossover. Therefore Blend 7/3 membrane was further investigated in depth by ex-situ accelerated stress test (AST) in conductivity and fuel cell test. As an AST, ex-situ conduc­tivity changes of Blend 7/3 membrane were recorded with a thermal cycling between different conditions of 160 ◦C (no humidity) and 80 ◦C (with 50 % relative humidity) as shown in Fig. 2 (e). The pPBI membrane has shown faster conductivity decay in 6 cycles at 160 ◦C losing more

Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091 than 70 % of its conductivity from the initial conductivity, while Blend 7/3 membrane lost less conductivity than pPBI after 26 thermal cycles. This higher conductivity retention of the blend membrane can be attributed to the stronger interaction between biphosphate and the pyridinium cation (ion-pair system), compared to the PA-PBI acid base interaction, even in the presence of water [30]. 3.6. Mechanical properties The mechanical properties of PA-doped commercial pPBI and MePVPyr/F6PBI blend membranes were investigated by means of their tensile strength (Fig. 2 (f)). The tensile strength is an important material feature to assess the applicability of the membranes for HT-PEMFC applications. It depends strongly on the measurement conditions such as temperature and humidity [11,31], therefore, the mechanical tests of all membranes were performed under the same conditions.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: rement conditions such as temperature and humidity [11,31], therefore, the mechanical tests of all membranes were performed under the same conditions. The mechanical property of blend membranes was consistent with the blend composi­tions, Blend 6/4 exhibited the highest tensile strength among tested membranes, followed by Blend 7/3 and Blend 8/2, corresponding to the decreased content of Me-PVPyr in the blend. All the blend membranes exhibited higher tensile strength than the pPBI membrane suggesting that those membranes could be assembled in a fuel cell. 3.7. Fuel cell test In the present study, a PA-doped Blend 7/3 (60 μm thickness)

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: Fig. 3. (a) Polarization curves of Blend 7/3 and pPBI MEA in H2/air FC at 160 ◦C; (b) 180 ◦C; (c) 200 ◦C; (d) current density at 0.25V at T = 20–200 ◦C, no back pressure and λ H2/air = 2/4; (e) polarization curves of Blend 7/3 membrane in H2/O2 FC without back pressure; (f) and with back pressure at 2 bars (g) and AST thermal cycling between 80 and 160 ◦C at 0.5 V, no back pressure and λ H2/air = 2/4. H. Cho et al. Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091 membrane was applied in HT-PEMFCs. The H2/air fuel cell performance of the MEA was compared with that of pPBI under the same conditions: T = 160, 180, or 200 ◦C, with dry gases (Fig. 3(a–c)). A lower open circuit voltage (OCV) was observed in Blend 7/3 MEA, which might be attributed to the higher gas cross-over or the relatively low MW of the pristine PVPyr. According to the literature, increasing the MW of the polymer (from 66 kDa to 170 kDa) significantly increases the OCV from 0.825 to 0.937V, using similar membrane thickness and doping levels [32]. Therefore, our future work will focus on preparing higher MW of PVPyr and applying it to PA doped HT-PEMFC to improve the OCV. Despite this, the Blend 7/3 MEA demonstrated better performance than a pPBI MEA at all tested temperatures. This enhancement is ascribed to the enhanced conductivity exhibited by the blend membrane in comparison to the pPBI membrane, as gauged in-situ during the FC's operational cycle as shown in Fig. S1.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: ductivity exhibited by the blend membrane in comparison to the pPBI membrane, as gauged in-situ during the FC's operational cycle as shown in Fig. S1. The impedance at high frequency intercepting the x-axis corresponds to the ohmic resistance of membrane (Zre). For the Blend 7/3 membrane, the ohmic resistance remained nearly constant in the temperature range of 160–200 ◦C, indicating stable proton conduction under elevated temperature conditions. In contrast, the pPBI membrane exhibited an increasing trend in ohmic resistance with increasing temperature. Specifically, the Blend 7/3 membrane maintained a low and stable Zre value of 17.2–17.4 mΩ from 160 ◦C to 200 ◦C, the Zre of the pPBI membrane increased markedly from 75.4 mΩ to 106.4 mΩ over the same temperature range. This result demonstrates the superior proton conduction characteristics of the Blend 7/3 membrane compared to the pPBI membrane in the fuel cell. In addition, the Blend 7/3 MEA exhibited the capacity to cold-start and operating at room temperature (Fig. 3(d) and Fig. S2), thereby obviating the necessity for an initial external heating apparatus to attain elevated operating temperatures. This is attributable to the exothermic nature of the electrochemical reaction in PEMFC, which results in the generation of substantial heat [33].

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: res. This is attributable to the exothermic nature of the electrochemical reaction in PEMFC, which results in the generation of substantial heat [33]. Thus, under optimal design parameters, a fuel cell stack possesses the capability to initiate operation and sustain functionality at low temperatures, thus maintaining this condition until the attainment of the desired operational temperature is achieved. The H2/O2 FC performance of the Blend 7/3 membrane was evalu­ated as shown in Fig. 3(e–f). Performance increased with temperature. The Blend 7/3 membrane exhibited little higher performance of 1.02 W cm-2 compared to commercial MEA (pPBI based commercial MEA) showing 0.97 W cm-2 under the same condition in H2/O2 FC at 160 ◦C without back pressure on the electrodes [34]. However, Blend 7/3 showed a superior performance displaying a maximum power density of 1.69 Wcm-2 obtained at 200 ◦C with 2 bars of back pressure on both anode and cathode. This represents the second-best global performance for the ion-pair-based MEA, utilizing, however, commercial electrodes. (Table 2). To investigate the membrane durability during FC operating, an accelerated stress test (AST) was conducted via thermal cycling between 80 and 160 ◦C (Fig. 3 (g)). In this AST, the current density of the pPBI MEA at 0.5 V gradually decreased with cycles (2.9 mA per cycle) due to the phosphoric acid loss, while Blend 7/3 MEA showed no significant drop of the performance.

Cross-section SEM images of the MEA after fuel cell test with Me-PVPyr/F6PBI blend membrane with 7/3 mass ratio at a) low and b) high magnification, c) contact area between PEM and CL: anode side and d) cathode side. e) EDX maps showing the distribution of phosphorus (P), nitrogen (N), platinum (Pt) and fluorine (F) within the MEA. (MEA - membrane electrode assembly, PEM - polymer electrolyte membrane, CL - catalyst layer, GDE - gas diffusion electrode). H. Cho et al. Journal of Membrane Science 742 (2026) 125091 performance of the Blend 7/3 MEA demonstrated a gradual enhance­ment over 70 cycles (3.0 mA per cycle), attributable to the membrane and electrode adaptation to the electrochemical processes [18]. It is important to note that the Blend 7/3 MEA has not undergone optimi­zation in a FC, as commercial electrodes were utilized without addi­tional modifications or optimization. This finding suggests the potential for further enhancement of FC performance. Therefore, considering the excellent performance of Blend 7/3 MEA in FC test, methylated poly­vinyl pyridinium would be a promising material for PA doped HT-PEMFCs when used with optimized electrodes. Subsequent to the completion of all fuel cell tests, including AST (~500 h: break-in for 3 days followed by all the tests in (Fig. 3), a postmortem analysis was conducted to examine morphological and compositional changes of the MEA prepared with Blend 7/3.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: all the tests in (Fig. 3), a postmortem analysis was conducted to examine morphological and compositional changes of the MEA prepared with Blend 7/3. This analysis utilized mostly scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in combi­nation with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis. The low magnification cross-section SEM image in Fig. 4 (a) reveals the sandwich structure of MEA, where the gas diffusion electrodes (GDEs) surround the proton electrolyte membrane (PEM) from both sides. A closer look at the MEA cross-section (Fig. 4 (b)) enables to well distin­guish the blend membrane from the catalyst layers (CLs) and allowed to define the membrane thickness, which vary along the membrane plane in the range of 10–15 μm. As visible on Fig. 4(c) and (d), despite the porous CL structure, the polymer membrane infiltrates within the su­perficial layer of the CL demonstrating a very good adhesion to the CL and high flexibility. The elemental mapping of both PEM and CL in Fig. 4 (e) showed the distribution of phosphorus (P) from PA and nitrogen (N) from the PVPyr component in the bland membrane, which are present mainly in the PEM, while platinum (Pt) and fluorine (F) are mainly observed in the CLs. This finding suggests that the membrane does not penetrate deeply into the CLs, which would otherwise lower the gas diffusion. The observed thinning of the membrane (from 60 μm down to ~ 15 μm) might be attributed to a membrane creeping effect [41].

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: se lower the gas diffusion. The observed thinning of the membrane (from 60 μm down to ~ 15 μm) might be attributed to a membrane creeping effect [41]. However, in this particular case, the membrane exhibits a tendency to creep along the lateral direction, albeit to a limited extent, with minimal penetration towards the electrodes. Nevertheless, both membrane thinning and the enhanced contact to the electrodes caused by creeping can positively influence the MEA performance. 4. Conclusions This study showed ion-pair blend membranes based on methylated PVPyr with F6PBI. The methylation on the PVPyr was quantitative and simply achieved. Due to the mechanical instability of Me-PVPyr alone, this needs to be blend with F6PBI to enhance the membranes stability. The blend ratio was optimized to be applied in PA doped HT-PEMFCs. Among the tested membranes, Blend 7/3 exhibited a proton conduc­tivity of 409 mS cm-1 at 180 ◦C, which is higher than that of a commercial PA doped pPBI membrane (366 mS cm-1) measured under anhydrous conditions. The Blend 7/3 membrane showed promising fuel cell performance, achieving a peak power density of 1.69 W cm-2 in H2/ O2 at 200 ◦C with 2 bars of back pressure on both anode and cathode sides. This performance was better than pPBI (Celltec membrane, commercially available PA doped membrane) under the same testing conditions. The thermal cycling performance from 80 to 160 ◦C by measuring a current density at 0.5 V was carried out as an AST test.

citation.title: Ion-pair membrane based on methylated poly(4-vinylpyridinium) for high temperature proton exchange membrane fuel cells

content: the same testing conditions. The thermal cycling performance from 80 to 160 ◦C by measuring a current density at 0.5 V was carried out as an AST test. In this test, the blend membrane showed no significant performance decay over 100 cycles, while pPBI has shown performance gradual decay within 70 cycles. These results indicate that the methylated PVPyr blend with F6PBI would be the promising membranes for PA doped HTPEMFCs.
